96d07277-ed2b-40ed-9b22-5dc4208a0e0d.comment: The illegally acquired wealth of Fidesz oligarchs must be confiscated!
The minimum wage should be made tax-free.
In cases where the state is not able to provide timely care, it may be justified to allow the use of private care at TB expense.
Subject to the fulfilment of the conditions.
We support not a complete ban, but reasonable restrictions.
Not sufficient as a standalone instrument.
Our programme includes a ban on the establishment of new battery factories and the closure of polluting battery factories.
Hungary's foreign policy and economic interests should be pursued within the European framework.
Subsidised loans are also needed, but not as the sole instrument.
State media in this form is not needed.
A work permit may only be issued if the labour demand cannot be met in the short term through retraining, and this is also approved by the relevant trade union/employee representation.

cbe2d0a1-1796-4b28-9350-53a1ea449cad.comment: As long as it cannot be replaced safely and stably, it is necessary in any case.
Or the voting age should be lowered to 16.

f3a1e92f-00ee-4024-bacf-820a7bdbf268.comment: In Hungary today, wealth concentration is so high that 1% of taxpayers hold 33.5% of net personal wealth. To address this problem, we have been advocating since our foundation for a wealth tax on oligarchs, because if this trend continues, the social scissors will not open further but will simply break. The wealth threshold subject to the oligarch tax must take into account property prices and inflation.

The currently entrenched political structure is not healthy, and in order to curb corruption it would be necessary to change the established system of power, but this should not be done with such an oversimplified rule.
Mi Hazánk supports increasing transparency in state operations, including the accountability of public funds and state contracts.

The state healthcare system has practically collapsed, which is why many people turn to private healthcare providers. It is reasonable to expect the state to cover non-luxury services in such cases.

Mi Hazánk does not believe the solution is for TB to cover a larger portion of private healthcare. Instead, the state healthcare system should be put in such an order that people only turn to private providers in exceptional cases.

Mi Hazánk Mozgalom supports the construction of Paks II and the further life extension of the Paks Nuclear Power Plant, and would in some circumstances continue and accelerate this, in compliance with enhanced nuclear safety requirements.

The heartbeat law was enacted following a proposal by Mi Hazánk. Mi Hazánk, standing on the ground of Christian and fundamental human morality, would like to give further opportunities for children at foetal age to live.
Mi Hazánk is a fully anti-drug party.

We will end state subsidy of content promoting deviance. Taxes, penalties and restrictions must stop the life-negating propaganda flooding us from commercial platforms. We also exclude any state-funded support for works containing LGBTQ propaganda!
Only in the traditional family model (male-female married couple).
Hungary must preserve its economic sovereignty, for which an independent currency is essential.

Mi Hazánk Mozgalom firmly rejects Ukraine's accession to the European Union and NATO. The party's position is that Ukraine's accession to the EU would economically devastate the union and Hungary too, and would mean pulling the war into the community.

A new referendum on EU membership should be held. If the "globalist" politics of the union continues, withdrawal could be a realistic alternative. We envisage a new European economic and interest group.

For us it is beyond question that small and medium-sized enterprises should form the backbone of the economy. Hungarian small and medium-sized enterprises should not receive less state support per job than foreign multinationals.
Our railway network and most of the rolling stock require immediate renovation or replacement.
Due to the advent of digitalisation and artificial intelligence, we would regulate the area within the framework of a digital child protection law.

Chemical castration of pedophile offenders! Crimes committed with particular cruelty against children should never become statute-barred, and their perpetrators should be permanently removed from society!

We hope that world politics will move towards peace. The increasing pressure of the arms industry lobby does not help bring this about. Although we consider it necessary to develop an army capable of independently defending the homeland, it is also not good to fuel the arms race. In the short term, however, a significant increase in the defence budget is certainly justified. It is unacceptable to continue spending tens of billions on participation in foreign occupations.

Crimes committed with particular cruelty against children should never become statute-barred, and their perpetrators should be permanently removed from society. The child protection system needs to be rebuilt. A digital child protection law is needed. The state must ensure that all services related to children are also available in analogue form.

The system needs to be completely overhauled. We link child benefit to work. From the third child onwards, we link all family support to work. Only those who can support children should have them, not those who want to live off them! Unfortunately, in many places children are born in large numbers not because of a love of large families or children, but because their parents see them as a potential source of income. Mi Hazánk would immediately establish a Family Planning Authority that would deal with individuals who demonstrably have children beyond their means. In addition to detailed information, the Family Planning Authority would provide free professional contraceptive devices so that children are not born in large numbers into deep deprivation. We will curb teenage motherhood and hold the parents of those who have children before the age of 18 accountable.

In order to preserve the value of pensions, we propose the reintroduction of Swiss indexation, which is currently only inflation-linked – if it can even be called that. We also propose the restoration of the service pension for law enforcement personnel.

Our party has been urging immediate water management for years. Our position is that the number of reservoirs should be radically increased, and that the existing canal network and water management facilities should be maintained and used not for draining water but for replenishing it!
Mi Hazánk would make wealth investigations mandatory for all politicians every four years.

We advocate the establishment of a Hungarian Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office. This Prosecutor's Office would operate on a parity basis with the participation of all parties, where every player has the right to request an investigation into another, thereby ensuring full coverage of the political spectrum and significant suppression of corruption.

Mi Hazánk calls for the immediate shutdown of all battery factories in Hungary, citing environmental burdens and risks to workers’ health. We demand that the government makes public all environmental impact studies of domestic battery plants.

In order to strengthen oversight of state management, the powers, organisational structure and budget of the State Audit Office should be radically expanded in proportion to the broader scope of competence and jurisdiction. The legal basis should be created for the Audit Office to be empowered to initiate criminal proceedings not only in the case of abuse but also in the case of uneconomical spending of public funds (e.g. in connection with overpriced, directed public procurement procedures).

Mi Hazánk Mozgalom calls for broad and thorough social consultation aimed at restoring our historical constitution based on the doctrine of the Holy Crown, for which a constitution drafted and adopted by a constituent national assembly representing the entire nation is needed, to replace the current Fundamental Law serving the interests of only one party.

Beyond this, we would tie the right to vote to at least basic literacy, and we have proposed a three-year custodial sentence and automatic exclusion from public affairs for those who sell their vote to others, and for those who buy other people's right to vote.
Mi Hazánk would immediately abolish the current profit-oriented enforcement system and replace it with a non-profit, state-run regulatory system.
National sovereignty is the goal. Hungary must represent independent foreign policy and economic interests, even if this differs from the position of the European Union.

Hungary is not energy self-sufficient and is in an import-dependent position with regard to energy sources. For supply security purposes, Mi Hazánk advocates the diversification of procurement options, which is why we also support steps to connect to the Southern Stream/TurkStream gas pipeline.

In the interests of young people and those most in need socially, we would launch a comprehensive housing programme, one key element of which would be the introduction, modelled on foreign examples, of a practice whereby after paying long-term rent for 25 years, ownership of the property would transfer to the tenant.

A new media law is needed that states that the media is an independent branch of power, that no domestic media outlet can be in foreign hands, and which ensures that publicly funded media cannot serve the interests of the current government exclusively, but should also give space to all parties in proportion to their actual support or the size of their parliamentary group.

Mi Hazánk would completely break with these practices and would protect, reclaim and preserve Hungarian land, because "whoever owns the land, owns the country"! The Hungarian countryside is our heartfelt concern, and we work for it to have not only a past but also a future!

A guest worker is also a migrant; in Western countries in the 1960s, immigration started the same way, and we can see where that led. Therefore we say no to guest workers imported into our country. We would provide tax incentives to companies that employ Hungarian workers instead of guest workers and reinvest a significant proportion of their profits into developments in Hungary.
Mi Hazánk would make income from overtime tax-free!
Rather, decent-paying jobs should be created instead of wages kept artificially low also due to guest workers.

We propose that home care provided by a family member be recognised as an employment relationship, with contribution payments, in a form similar to childcare benefit. This would prevent elderly family members from automatically ending up in care homes, and would also ease the financial difficulties of the carer.
In education, instead of forcing integration at all costs, segregation is needed in certain cases. We would make it possible for those who are unable to comply with standards to leave the schools.
The quality of state education must be significantly raised, but private schools cannot be excluded, nor can segregation.
It should definitely be raised somewhat, since the age of 16 is not sufficient for acquiring a vocational qualification.

af78f800-49d1-4239-8ea7-0bc2d74639b5.comment: “An annual 1% wealth tax will be introduced on the portion of wealth above 1 billion forints for those who have assets exceeding 1 billion forints.” (K-Monitor)
“We will limit the prime minister’s mandate to two terms.” (K-Monitor)
“We will reduce the tax on the minimum wage from 15 percent to 9 percent.” (K-Monitor)

"TISZA did not make a promise regarding this, but in their program they commit that "state and private property transfer and option contracts above a specified threshold will be made available online in the National Contract Repository." (K-Monitor)
TISZA have not taken a public position on the issue.

TISZA has not taken a specific position on the issue, but it follows from the program that they would not expand the scope of privately provided services financed by social security: "we will ensure access for everyone to free, high-quality public healthcare." (K-Monitor)
"We are committed to ensuring Hungary’s growing electricity consumption through the construction of a safe nuclear power plant." (K-Monitor)

TISZA has not taken a public position on the issue, but at a women’s rights conference Péter Magyar, when asked whether Hungary’s abortion regulations are acceptable, stated: “I think they are. But it is not for me to decide this. [...] I believe there are social issues such as euthanasia or abortion that should be debated by society.” (K-Monitor)

TISZA has not made any specific reference to drug liberalization, but their program rather implies a rejection of drug liberalization: “In Hungary [...] we will implement an effective, strict but human-centered drug policy...” (K-Monitor)
TISZA has not taken a public position on the issue.

“We will abolish the restriction on adoption by single people.
We will make it possible for foster parents to adopt without administrative barriers; our goal is for every child, if possible, to grow up in a family.” (K-Monitor)
"We will prepare for the introduction of the euro and implement it with a foreseeable target date." (K-Monitor)

"We do not support the accelerated accession of Ukraine to the European Union. If, after many years, all accession chapters are closed, Hungary will hold a decisive, legally binding referendum on the issue." (K-Monitor)
"We reaffirm our commitment to Hungary’s membership in the European Union and NATO, as well as to the international rules-based order." (K-Monitor)
"The focus of support policy will be on Hungarian small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as the industries of the future." (K-Monitor)

"Our highway network, built at an exorbitant cost and burdened with corruption, is of European standard, while the railway network, main and secondary roads, as well as many elements of public transport, fall short of the expected level." (K-Monitor)

"We will launch a comprehensive digital education and parental support program so that the protection of children is based not solely on prohibition, but on knowledge, preparation, and cooperation." (K-Monitor)
"We will provide full financial and moral compensation for victims who have suffered abuse in the child protection system." (K-Monitor)
"We will increase our defense spending to the NATO-required level of 5%, which we will achieve by 2035, at a pace that the strength of the Hungarian economy allows." (K-Monitor)

"The current system of family support is incapable of offsetting the costs associated with having children, and thus does not encourage childbearing.

The current family support system benefits the more affluent social groups the most, while those in greatest need receive little assistance." (K-Monitor)

TISZA has not made a specific commitment to introducing a mixed indexation system, but instead promised nominal increases in pensions.

"We guarantee that no one will receive less than 120,000 forints per month in old-age and disability pensions. This will significantly increase the pensions of more than 280,000 pensioners.

We will also significantly raise pensions between 120,000 and 140,000 forints: this will mean an additional 6,000–12,000 forints per month for 140,000 pensioners. Pensions above 140,000 forints will be increased in a differentiated manner." (K-Monitor)

"We view water not as a problem, but as a solution: our goal is the “rehydration” of Hungary, restoring the landscape’s water retention capacity.

We will improve water retention in the Homokhátság region." (K-Monitor)
"We will introduce asset investigations going back 20 years for members of parliament, political leaders, and their close family members." (K-Monitor)
"We will join the European Public Prosecutor’s Office." (K-Monitor)
"We will establish an independent body to continuously monitor heavily polluting industries and activities, with a particular focus on battery industry investments." (K-Monitor)

TISZA’s programme does not make a commitment regarding disclosure obligations for private companies. “To improve the transparency of public procurement, we will further develop the Electronic Public Procurement System, and ensure the public and easy accessibility of public procurement documents, bidder data, and final contracts.” (K-Monitor)
"We will develop a constitution proposal that reunifies the nation through broad social consultation." (K-Monitor)

"We will unify the voting rights and voting procedures of Hungarians living beyond the borders and our compatriots in the diaspora. We will expand the number of polling stations available abroad. We will examine the possibility of online voting." (K-Monitor)
"We will place enforcement and liquidation under the courts." (K-Monitor)

"Anita Orbán, TISZA’s foreign policy expert, stated regarding Hungary’s EU role: “We do not want to be a stick between the spokes, but a spoke on the wheel.” The party’s program also addresses the issue: “We will stop the process of drifting away from the European Union and become a respected member of the community, constructively shaping it with our views and acting as a guiding member.” (K-Monitor)
"We will eliminate dependence on Russian energy by 2035 and diversify the country’s energy supply." (K-Monitor)

TISZA’s program places greater emphasis on developing a state housing system and takes a critical stance toward the current credit-based housing support system: “Due to the misguided housing policy of recent years, rental prices and housing prices have skyrocketed; since 2010 they have tripled, while wages have not kept pace.” (K-Monitor)
"We will restore the independence and political neutrality of state media, and we will adopt a new media law." (K-Monitor)

"Szabolcs Bóna, TISZA’s agricultural expert, stated: “The amendment of the land law is only one step in a coordinated attack aimed at dispossessing farmers.”

The program emphasizes the development of Hungary’s agricultural and food industry, but does not make specific statements about treating land as an investment asset. (K-Monitor)

[Szabolcs Bóna's page](https://www.facebook.com/bonaszabolcstisza/posts/pfbid02UCqZrqY1woxNKZmcP31VKbrCoSXy5vGT5Ds3cAJYVyuzj7abRD5JwUVg2Vh9JeSml)
[https://cdn.tisza.work/A működő és emberséges Magyarország alapjai.pdf#page=218](Tisza's program)

"From June 1, 2026, until further notice, we will prohibit the mass import of non-Hungarian guest workers from outside the European Union. We will not allow foreign guest workers to take jobs from Hungarian workers or to drive down wages." (K-Monitor)

TISZA’s program does not state whether it would tighten regulations through legislation, but it places significant emphasis on employee well-being.

"We will encourage collective agreements to cover not only wages, but also training, working conditions, and work–life balance."
"In the public sector and at state-owned companies, we will set an example with modern work organization to create attractive, predictable workplaces."
"We will review the Labour Code and strengthen workers’ ability to assert their interests." (K-Monitor)

"We will consistently enforce the principle of equal pay for equal work. In the field of employment, we will introduce and consistently apply laws on wage transparency. We will enshrine equal pay in legislation. Through regular inspections and the application of sanctions, we will act against gender pay gaps." (K-Monitor)

TISZA’s program does not include a plan to increase or modify unemployment benefits; the focus is instead on active labor market interventions.

"We will support the spread of part-time and flexible employment forms, as well as remote work, through regulation and financial incentives, along with the employment of people with reduced work capacity and the creation of caring workplaces."
"Using active labor market tools (training, retraining, employment services) and comprehensive catch-up programs, we will encourage the employment of low-skilled and disadvantaged social groups." (K-Monitor)

The program mentions developing social care and elderly care, but instead of classifying home care as employment, it proposes increasing the current allowances.

"We will increase home care allowances by 50%." (K-Monitor)
We will begin eliminating segregated education, and by restoring the freedom of the education system, we will enable increasingly personalized and community-tailored forms of education. (K-Monitor)
The program deals in detail with the development of education, but does not make a clear statement about the role and impact of private schools. (K-Monitor)
"We will restore the autonomy of universities and abolish the KEKVA model." (K-Monitor)
"We will raise the compulsory school age to 18 and ensure that Roma young people leaving the school system have knowledge that is useful in real life." (K-Monitor)
Municipalities will receive substantive competencies in local public services, development policy, environmental protection, and shaping the urban landscape. (K-Monitor)

cf177ab5-1e70-40a7-afa0-afb2089259f0.comment: In a 2025 interview, Viktor Orbán rejected the concept of a wealth tax, stating: "Let us try not to bother people, not walk around in their homes, not demand tax returns from them, not check where they go on holiday or what cars they have, but let them work." (K-Monitor)
The government has taken no steps to cap the number of prime ministerial terms; Viktor Orbán stated at the 2023 Kötcse picnic that he plans to govern until 2034. (K-Monitor)

The Fidesz-KDNP government introduced the flat-rate personal income tax after its 2010 election victory and has argued in its favour on numerous occasions since. In September 2025, Viktor Orbán claimed that progressive taxation is "inherently bureaucratic, cumbersome and full of tricks". (K-Monitor)

Since 2010, the Fidesz government has classified numerous contracts on national security or business interest grounds, including those for the construction of Paks II and the renovation of the Budapest-Belgrade railway. Furthermore, although the government recently created a contract database (kif.gov.hu), it contains only metadata, not the actual contracts. (K-Monitor)

In 2024, the Ministry of the Interior responded to a petition by Patent Association that it does not plan to change the current regulation, and parliament did not place the issue on its agenda. (K-Monitor)

Fidesz has not committed to opening social insurance to services in private healthcare. Péter Takács, State Secretary of Healthcare stated in 2025 that this is not planned, declaring: "Those private providers who offer specialised care are already within the TB coverage, and there are no plans to open the fund to new players." (K-Monitor)

The development of the Paks nuclear power plant was one of the most defining investments of the Fidesz-KDNP governance. According to Viktor Orbán, without nuclear energy "there is no successful Hungarian economy, and there is no affordable electricity for households either." (K-Monitor)
The government introduced the heartbeat law in 2022, with Gergely Gulyás claiming they were following the position of the "professional college". (K-Monitor)

The Fidesz government has long been opposed to drug legalisation efforts and has tightened drug regulations on multiple occasions. In late January 2026, the prime minister stated that "[...] we will never accept anyone legalising or making us accept drugs." (K-Monitor)

Fidesz has repeatedly rejected same-sex marriage; in 2016, Viktor Orbán stated that "[...] only a man and a woman can marry and start a family." The Fidesz government amended the Fundamental Law in 2020 to state: "The mother is a woman, the father is a man." (K-Monitor)

Since 2021, singles in Hungary have only been able to adopt with ministerial permission, as a result of which the proportion of single adopters has dropped significantly, while the possibility has practically ceased entirely for same-sex couples. (K-Monitor)

The Fidesz government has repeatedly opposed plans to introduce the euro since 2010. Viktor Orbán last spoke about the issue in 2025, when he said: “The introduction of the euro is certainly not on my agenda.” (K-Monitor)

Fidesz strongly opposes Ukraine’s EU accession, and the prime minister has also repeatedly expressed his reservations on the matter. In March 2026, Viktor Orbán said, “if Ukraine were to join the Union, all the money of Hungarians would go to Ukraine, and the amount of contributions would also increase.” (K-Monitor)
Although Fidesz is a vocal critic of the European Union, the Fidesz-led government has repeatedly expressed its commitment to EU membership. (K-Monitor)

The system of individual subsidies and tax incentives forms the basis of the economic policy of the Fidesz government, and several Fidesz politicians have already praised the favorable economic results of these programs. At a factory opening in 2025, Viktor Orbán explained the necessity of the system by saying, “these investments make the future predictable and secure, so that there are well-paying jobs, a predictable career path, a family, a home, children, that we can keep taxes low, and that there is a fair pension.” (K-Monitor)

The mobility policy of Fidesz-KDNP has been defined by the priority of road network development. According to the Fidesz government's 2024 plans, HUF 16,000 billion would be spent on roads while only HUF 4,000 billion would be allocated on railway development over the next 10 years. (K-Monitor)

Although Fidesz has not made any specific commitment to ban social media use by minors, several politicians have mentioned the issue as a problem. On 23 October 2025, Viktor Orbán called on young people to "Get off the machine!", and in early 2026, Máriusz Révész, State Secretary for Active Hungary, developed a package of proposals that would ban social media use for those under 14. (K-Monitor)

The Fidesz government has never promised to establish a compensation fund for those harmed within the child protection system. At the end of 2025, Gergely Gulyás stated regarding the government’s work: “What could be done in the interest of child protection has been done.” (K-Monitor)

Viktor Orbán has spoken inconsistently about raising military spending. At the end of 2024, he said raising it to 5% would "shoot the Hungarian economy in the lungs", but less than six months later he found the target achievable if the EU relaxed its budgetary rules. Hungary supported the 5% decision of the Hague NATO summit, but the Fidesz government has not yet put this into practice, so implementation remains uncertain. (K-Monitor)

The family tax allowance system is one of the most defining elements of Fidesz government policy, with almost every Fidesz politician speaking about its advantages. Balázs Hankó put it as follows: "Hungary is family-friendly because the government does not give money to families with children but leaves the tax allowances with them." (K-Monitor)

After the Fidesz-KDNP came to power in 2010, they phased out the mixed indexation system. Since the pension reform, the Fidesz government has not committed to restoring the old system, focusing instead on introducing the 13th and 14th monthly pension. (K-Monitor)

The Fidesz government adopted a new water management strategy in 2025, but made no specific statement about the urgency of desertification. Viktor Orbán last touched on the topic in 2023 during a parliamentary debate, saying that more water enters the country than leaves it and "Hungary suffers from no water shortage." (K-Monitor)
In 2015, the Fidesz government amended the law so that the tax authority would only be able to conduct wealth investigations in very rare circumstances. (K-Monitor)

According to Fidesz–KDNP, the prosecution service is a “sovereignty issue,” and Brussels, by establishing the European Public Prosecutor’s Office, seeks to gain even more power for itself at the expense of the member states. (K-Monitor)

The strategy of the Fidesz government is to make Hungary an “accumulator manufacturing superpower,” which is why encouraging investment is a key economic policy goal. For this reason, their communication emphasises that the regulations are already sufficiently strict and that investments are important for the economy and for jobs. According to the Fidesz government, battery factories operate “under strict environmental regulations.” Fidesz politicians often say that the opposition is “spreading fear” about battery factories, while these facilities create jobs and contribute to economic growth. (K-Monitor)

The Constitutional Court called on parliament to amend the freedom of information law in order to make companies that receive public funds but do not respond to data requests sueable. Despite this, the Fidesz government has not taken action in the matter for more than five years. (K-Monitor)
The currently valid Fundamental Law was adopted with the support of Fidesz-KDNP; they naturally recognise it as legitimate and do not consider it necessary to change it. (K-Monitor)
It was Fidesz that introduced the rule allowing citizens without a registered address in Hungary to vote by post (since 2014). (K-Monitor)

In Hungary, the bailiff system is currently not state-owned, but is carried out by independent enforcement officers (under state supervision). Although there have been corruption scandals, the Fidesz government did not nationalise the system but rather made personnel and institutional changes. In a 2023 parliamentary debate, Viktor Orbán stated: "It is not beneficial, not viable and not workable to carry out enforcement on a non-profit basis." (K-Monitor)

“Sovereignty” and “national interest” are key concepts in Fidesz’s foreign policy. The Fidesz-led government has on several occasions prevented or blocked EU decisions, for example by vetoing measures related to Russia or support for Ukraine. Viktor Orbán regularly emphasizes that Hungary makes decisions based on its own national interests, even when they do not align with the majority of the EU. (K-Monitor)

The Fidesz government has stated on multiple occasions that Hungary cannot do without Russian energy in the short to medium term. Viktor Orbán has said that Russian energy is the foundation of Hungary's energy system and that without it, energy prices and economic costs would increase drastically. Hungary is one of the most dependent countries on Russian energy in the EU, and the Fidesz government opposes several EU plans that would require rapid disengagement. (K-Monitor)

Central elements of the Fidesz government's housing policy are subsidised loans (e.g. the CSOK loan, baby loan, 3% housing loan). Their goal is for families to obtain housing through loans. (K-Monitor)

Hungarian state media is currently supervised by governmental media institutions (e.g. MTVA and the National Media and Infocommunications Authority), whose leadership is influenced by political appointments. International organisations frequently criticise the independence of public media. (K-Monitor)

On Christmas 2025, the Fidesz government permitted the purchase of agricultural land for leasing purposes, thereby effectively clearing the way for those wishing to buy agricultural land primarily for investment rather than cultivation purposes. (K-Monitor)
From 2025, the Fidesz government has reduced the guest worker quota to 35,000, but continues to provide the possibility for third-country guest workers to come to Hungary. (K-Monitor)
In 2018, the Fidesz government adopted an amendment to the Labour Code that significantly increased the number of permitted overtime hours. (K-Monitor)

Currently, there are no regulations specifying any sanctions for employers who pay lower wages to their female employees. Katalin Novák (former minister responsible for families and later President of Hungary) stated that it is not a problem if a woman receives lower pay for her work in the same position. (K-Monitor)

The policy of the Fidesz government has for years been to keep unemployment benefits available for a short period and instead encourage a quick return to work. In Hungary, unemployment benefits currently last for a maximum of 3 months, which is one of the shortest durations in the EU. (K-Monitor)
Parliament's governing majority rejected consideration of the proposal. (K-Monitor)

Although the Fidesz government took legislative action against the phenomenon in 2023, according to experts the Fidesz-KDNP education policy has strengthened segregation. Previously, Zoltán Balog, Minister of EMMI, spoke of "loving separation" leading to better education. (K-Monitor)

The Fidesz government does not restrict the operation of private schools and does not claim that they are harmful to public education. Rather, it emphasises free choice of institution and has in several cases financially supported private schools. (K-Monitor)
The Fidesz government introduced the so-called university model change, during which many state universities were transferred to foundation management. (K-Monitor)
Under the Fidesz government in 2012, the compulsory school age was reduced from 18 to 16. (K-Monitor)

In recent years, the Fidesz government has centralised several important public services, i.e. decision-making and management have passed from municipalities to the state. This suggests that the government prefers stronger state management over greater municipal autonomy. (K-Monitor)
